Monday, 03. November 2025

IdM Thoughtplace

Schemas and Some Elements of LDAP History

Note: all trademarks mentioned in this blog are the property of respective owners. I've been meaning to write this entry in one form or another for over 20 years. Glad I finally got around to it. LDAP has been around since 1993, while Microsoft's Active Directory was introduced in 2000. During this time, Active Directory has become a virtually universal constant in organizations worldwide. Appro

Note: all trademarks mentioned in this blog are the property of respective owners.

I've been meaning to write this entry in one form or another for over 20 years. Glad I finally got around to it.

LDAP has been around since 1993, while Microsoft's Active Directory was introduced in 2000. During this time, Active Directory has become a virtually universal constant in organizations worldwide. Approximately 90% of the Fortune 1000 use it. It's hard to escape from it. But there is a definite appeal to setting up additional Directory Service instances from Microsoft or other providers. Setting up these additional instances helps to properly segregate different user types (employees, customers, vendors, etc.) and, particularly in the Active Directory use case, helps manage licenses and keep all OS and Application infrastructure accessible by Active Directory out of prying hands.

Herein lies the issue at hand. For its own reasons, Microsoft does not use the same object classes as standard LDAP. For those unfamiliar with LDAP, an object class is a grouping of attributes. Object classes facilitate the definition of users, groups, and other components of the LDAP structure, thereby introducing some organization to the overall schema.

Standard LDAP uses the inetOrgPerson as the basic definition of a user, while Active Directory uses the user. Most of this grew from the basic organization of Active Directory, along with the additional information required by integrating Microsoft Exchange back when it was an on-premises application. Of course, as the two concepts evolved, differences cropped up that I need to reference from time to time. To make this easier, I'm listing the most important differences here. I've listed the standard LDAP attribute first, followed by the Active Directory attribute.

jpegPhoto -- thumbnailPhoto secretary -- assistant street    -- streetAddress uid       -- typically not used I'm sure there are a few others, and I can see updating this list as things change in the future. Also, the uid attribute is typically used in Active Directory when it is being synchronized with a more standards-based LDAP, as it usually uses uid as the primary identifier as opposed to cn when building the entry's distinguishedName. It's also important to remember that the value stored in userPassword is not encrypted, but rather it is a hashed representation of the password. (This article provides a nice description of the process) This means that there is no way to decode this value and setting it typically requires an SSL connection.

One final note here, I've pointed out some differences between Active Directory and LDAP. This is not necessarily a criticism of Active Directory. Active Directory is a proprietary evolution of the original LDAP standard for some of the reasons I mentioned above and more. Standard LDAP is just that, LDAP based applications more closely adhering to RFC2798 and typically being a "descendent" of the original AOL-Netscape-Sun Directory Server code.

Let me know if you have other attributes that should be added to this list.

22:02 • 6 months ago

Jon Udell

Release the Kraken!

Tuscon’s Museum of Miniatures features hundreds of exhibits like this one. “Artist Madelyn Cook spent over 3 years planning and constructing Lagniappe, which includes two separate wings and 25 individual rooms.” People have been making these for hundreds of years, but in recent decades practioners have become more precise about measurement and scale. Many of … Continue reading Release the Kraken!

Tuscon’s Museum of Miniatures features hundreds of exhibits like this one.

“Artist Madelyn Cook spent over 3 years planning and constructing Lagniappe, which includes two separate wings and 25 individual rooms.”

People have been making these for hundreds of years, but in recent decades practioners have become more precise about measurement and scale. Many of the exhibits use a 1:12 (inch:foot) ratio.

“Cook chose to portray the estate of a fictional merchant sea captain and his family living during the American colonial period.”

The fine detail is mind boggling. See that page on the desk above? You can actually read it.


There are rooms full of these installations, many of which date from the 1980s and 1990s when an American community of practice coalesced around the style.

“4 room Rococo château, with furnishings inspired by European palaces such as those of Seville and Versailles. Designed and created by Schoenbach, of Atlanta, Georgia, over a 30-year period.”

I would guess that the whole collection representions millions of hours of effort. It’s almost overwhelming to contemplate.

This guy, Salavat Fidai, sculpts pencil tips. His medium is not quite as insane as that of Willard Wigan, whose work I saw at The Museum of Jurassic Technology. But it pushes the envelope.


As amazing as these miniatures are, I might not have made the visit just to see them. The tractor beam that pulled me in was the special exhibit of Ray Harryhausen’s orginal animatronic models and drawings. Here’s the Kraken from Clash of the Titans.


According to the Harryhausen Foundation’s podcast, he took creative liberties when bringing the legends to life. For example, this scene is a mashup of Jason and the Argonauts and the Labors of Hercules. It was actually Hercules who fought the Hydra. This bothered some classicists but Harryhausen was a pragmatist: “We have to manipulate certain aspects in order to make a movie that will flow.”


Who doesn’t love Bubo the mechanical owl?


American censors, however, did not love bare-breasted Medusa, though they were perfectly fine with her violent and bloody decapitation. Europeans, unsurprisingly, had the inverse reaction.

The skeletons from the iconic swordfighting scene were smaller than I imagined.


This model is from a film I never heard of.


The sign says:

The Story of the Tortoise and the Hare

Ray Harryhausen

c. 1952

This is the original model, rediscovered in 2008. An identical replica was made in 2002 to complete this unfinished film, 50 years later.

In 2002, Seamus Walsh and Mark Caballero of Screen Novelties, the award-winning American stop-motion animation studio, worked with Ray Harryhausen to complete his final fairy tale film, The Story of the Tortoise and the Hare, which Ray began in 1953 and never finished. Ray was delighted and grateful for their assistance and greatly admired how Mark and Seamus were able to seamlessly blend the new and original footage.


You can see the remarkable collection of miniatures anytime. But the Harryhausen exhibit, which arrived in Tuscon in September and leaves next May, is a rare U.S. appearance of artifacts that normally reside in Scotland. (Why? Ray’s wife, Diana, had very strong links to Scotland, being the great-great granddaughter of explorer David Livingstone.) So visit soon if you can!


05:45 • 6 months ago

Friday, 31. October 2025

Jon Udell

A day in Sequoia National Park

Exactly one hundred and fifty years ago John Muir walked around in the same grove of giant sequoia trees that I walked around in today, and stood next to the same two thousand ton behemoth that had been growing for two and a half millenia. It has only been known as the General Sherman tree … Continue reading A day in Sequoia National Park

Exactly one hundred and fifty years ago John Muir walked around in the same grove of giant sequoia trees that I walked around in today, and stood next to the same two thousand ton behemoth that had been growing for two and a half millenia.

It has only been known as the General Sherman tree for a tiny fraction of its immense lifespan. I imagine it standing there blissfully unaware of its association with a cruel and destructive human being, indeed unaware of any human activity at all.

But we are making our presence known.


“Death of large sequoias (over 4 ft in diameter) in wildfires prior to 2015 was very rare”

This was my first trip to Sequoia National Park. I explored the tiny section shown on this 1927 USGS topological map.

(Wikipedia)

It’s worth clicking through to the high-res version, zooming in, and imagining what it was like to reach that place in 1875 before there were roads and cars never mind GPS-connected handheld computers.

On the Congress trail in this densest of Sequoiadendron giganteum groves, other magnificent specimens suffer comparison to notable Americans, most painfully this cluster called The House. (There’s a Senate too.)

I live among coast redwoods and was delighted to finally meet their shorter and stouter cousins. If you’ve been thinking about a visit, know that the park is open but unstaffed. I only saw one ranger and he was on latrine duty, nobody is collecting the entrance fee, yet another bit of economic fallout from the shutdown.

After walking the Congress trail I headed down to the museum (which is closed), hiked over to Moro Rock, and walked up the steps to take in the view.

(Wikipedia)

Someday I hope to ascend Half Dome using the cable hand rails but this was an easy way to enjoy the view from a big granite dome. Whitney is only a dozen miles away but “the Great Western Divide rises high enough to block it”.

My day started in Three Rivers and ended in Tehachapi after a long and rewarding detour into another section of the park.

The road up to Lake Isabella winds gradually through Sierra foothills that seemed mellower and more mesmerizing than the ones I’ve seen farther north. The road down follows the Kern River as it flows over endless pillows of granite. There’s nothing like a big dose of the majesty of California, a friend likes to say. It sure was powerful medicine today.

05:42 • 6 months ago

Thursday, 30. October 2025

Mike Jones: self-issued

Fully-Specified Algorithms for JOSE and COSE is now RFC 9864

The “Fully-Specified Algorithms for JSON Object Signing and Encryption (JOSE) and CBOR Object Signing and Encryption (COSE)” specification has been published as RFC 9864! I believe that this is the first RFC I’ve worked on that started its journey as a presentation of an idea to the working group without an accompanying draft. The idea […]

The “Fully-Specified Algorithms for JSON Object Signing and Encryption (JOSE) and CBOR Object Signing and Encryption (COSE)” specification has been published as RFC 9864! I believe that this is the first RFC I’ve worked on that started its journey as a presentation of an idea to the working group without an accompanying draft. The idea was well received by the JOSE Working Group at IETF 117 in July 2023 and so Orie Steele and I took the next step of writing a draft. The work was done in close coordination with the COSE Working Group.

The abstract from the RFC describes its contributions as follows:

This specification refers to cryptographic algorithm identifiers that fully specify the cryptographic operations to be performed, including any curve, key derivation function (KDF), and hash functions, as being “fully specified”. It refers to cryptographic algorithm identifiers that require additional information beyond the algorithm identifier to determine the cryptographic operations to be performed as being “polymorphic”. This specification creates fully-specified algorithm identifiers for registered JSON Object Signing and Encryption (JOSE) and CBOR Object Signing and Encryption (COSE) polymorphic algorithm identifiers, enabling applications to use only fully-specified algorithm identifiers. It deprecates those polymorphic algorithm identifiers.

This specification updates RFCs 7518, 8037, and 9053. It deprecates polymorphic algorithms defined by RFCs 8037 and 9053 and provides fully-specified replacements for them. It adds to the instructions to designated experts in RFCs 7518 and 9053.

This is one that the world has been wanting and waiting for! There are already normative references to it both from IETF specs and also W3C, FIDO Alliance, and OpenID Foundation specifications.

I’m particularly proud of this one because it not only fixes the real and present problem of polymorphic algorithm identifiers that has plagued implementations and systems; it also ensures that the problem cannot recur, by mandating that only fully-specified algorithm identifiers can henceforth be registered. In my view, this one makes the world better.

Tuesday, 28. October 2025

Talking Identity

Why I joined the FIDO Alliance

I’m back from an exhilarating, albeit tiring, week at the FIDO Alliance’s annual Authenticate conference. As you probably already know, I recently joined the Alliance as Chief Technology Officer, which made this experience a little bit different than years past. There is a lot going on in the digital identity ecosystem right now, which really […]

I’m back from an exhilarating, albeit tiring, week at the FIDO Alliance’s annual Authenticate conference. As you probably already know, I recently joined the Alliance as Chief Technology Officer, which made this experience a little bit different than years past. There is a lot going on in the digital identity ecosystem right now, which really drives why I took on this new role, a question that many have asked me over the last couple of months.

If you’ve followed my conference talks and blog posts, you know I care deeply about one simple idea: security should make people feel safer and let them get on with their lives. The FIDO Alliance has been driven by that mission for years, with passkeys being the result of that drive – phishing-resistant authentication that is user-friendly and now broadly available across platforms.

But the road from specifications to success has two lanes: adoption at scale and alignment with the rules of the road. Adoption means helping implementers make the right choices, smoothing out the rough edges of real deployments, and proving interoperability through strong certification programs. Alignment means working with policymakers and regulators so that what’s good security practice is also recognized in frameworks and guidance around the world. Both require patient, collaborative work. That’s a big part of why I’m here, and what I find exciting and motivating about taking on this new challenge.

My role at FIDO will be to help our membership guide the Alliance’s technical strategy, keep our specifications coherent and practical, and make sure they tie cleanly to certification and interoperability so relying parties can trust what they’re deploying. It also means rolling up sleeves with our members, other standards bodies, and the public sector to ensure FIDO technology fits naturally into the broader digital trust fabric.

That fabric itself is evolving quite rapidly. Authentication doesn’t live in a vacuum; it connects to how identities are verified, how credentials are carried, and how transactions move. As the ecosystem matures, we’ll continue doing what the FIDO Alliance has always done best: focus on pragmatic, widely adoptable building blocks that make it harder to phish people, harder to steal value, and easier for legitimate users to get things done. This will necessarily touch adjacent areas – identity verification, wallets, payments flows – where clear interfaces and strong assurances help reduce breach and fraud. The point isn’t to broaden the mission for its own sake, but to finish the mission we started: making the online world safer and simpler for everyone.

04:17 • 6 months ago

Saturday, 25. October 2025

Jon Udell

Reimagining car culture

The Volts podcast continues to be my favorite listen. Climate change will wreak ever more havoc on the world, that’s just baked in. But the transition to clean energy is also now baked in. David Roberts delivers a steady stream of hopeful news on that front: plummeting prices for solar panels and batteries, “reconductoring” to … Continue reading Reimagining car culture

The Volts podcast continues to be my favorite listen. Climate change will wreak ever more havoc on the world, that’s just baked in. But the transition to clean energy is also now baked in. David Roberts delivers a steady stream of hopeful news on that front: plummeting prices for solar panels and batteries, “reconductoring” to grow the capacity of the existing grid, agrivoltaics, new geothermal techniques, and much more.

Cars are a big part of the story. Switching to EVs is great but if we only do that we are still stuck with too many large heavy vehicles that clog roads when moving, waste vast amounts of space when parked, and harm people who move through the world on foot or on bicycles. We don’t just want cleaner cars, we also want far fewer of them. This episode, with the authors of Life After Cars, explores the “tyranny of the automobile”.

American car culture always seemed wrong to me, for many reasons. On this show David Roberts crystallized one of them.

When you ride a bike through Amsterdam, you are a dozen times every minute making small adjustments to other people, and you are accommodating yourself and coordinating with other people in these micro ways over and over and over again as you ride through Amsterdam.

And it just has an effect. You realize you’re living among other people and you’re involved in a common project and you live in a common place and you’re together in the place.

I have long been fascinated by a video called A trip down Market Street. Filmed in San Francisco in 1906, shortly before the great quake, it’s a long shot that moves down Market Street toward the Ferry Building. You see a free-for-all of trolleys, pedestrians, bicycles, horsedrawn carriages, and cars. Clearly the cars are going to win but in this moment they are not yet hermetically sealed shells, they have open tops so drivers see one another and make the same kinds of micro-adjustments to cyclists and pedestrians.

In a San Franciso with fewer and more autonomous cars, can we imagine a way to recapture that kind of sociality?

Friday, 17. October 2025

Mike Jones: self-issued

OpenID Federation draft 44 Incorporating Features Motivated by Swedish Government Use Cases

Draft 44 of the OpenID Federation specification has been published. The draft contains improved descriptions of a number of features. The one breaking change made is that Trust Mark Status responses are now signed. Some of the changes made are intended to facilitate implementation of features needed for some Swedish government use cases. In particular, […]

Draft 44 of the OpenID Federation specification has been published. The draft contains improved descriptions of a number of features. The one breaking change made is that Trust Mark Status responses are now signed.

Some of the changes made are intended to facilitate implementation of features needed for some Swedish government use cases. In particular, extension points were added to make it easier to use OpenID Federation for trust establishment for systems where existing entities may already be deployed, and may not be able to be modified.

The changes made in -44 are detailed in the Document History section.

Thanks all for the continued progress towards finishing the specification!

03:03 • 7 months ago

Saturday, 11. October 2025

Aaron Parecki

Adding Support for BlueSky to IndieLogin.com

Today I just launched support for BlueSky as a new authentication option in IndieLogin.com!

Today I just launched support for BlueSky as a new authentication option in IndieLogin.com!

IndieLogin.com is a developer service that allows users to log in to a website with their domain. It delegates the actual user authentication out to various external services, whether that is an IndieAuth server, GitHub, GitLab, Codeberg, or just an email confirmation code, and now also BlueSky.

This means if you have a custom domain as your BlueSky handle, you can now use it to log in to websites like indieweb.org directly!

Alternatively, you can add a link to your BlueSky handle from your website with a rel="me atproto" attribute, similar to how you would link to your GitHub profile from your website.

<a href="https://example.bsky.social" rel="me atproto">example.bsky.social</a>

Full setup instructions here

This is made possible thanks to BlueSky's support of the new OAuth Client ID Metadata Document specification, which was recently adopted by the OAuth Working Group. This means as the developer of the IndieLogin.com service, I didn't have to register for any BlueSky API keys in order to use the OAuth server! The IndieLogin.com website publishes its own metadata which the BlueSky OAuth server can use to fetch the metadata from. This is the same client metadata that an IndieAuth server will parse as well! Aren't standards fun!

The hardest part about the whole process was probably adding DPoP support. Actually creating the DPoP JWT wasn't that bad but the tricky part was handling the DPoP server nonces sent back. I do wish we had a better solution for that mechanism in DPoP, but I remember the reasoning for doing it this way and I guess we just have to live with it now.

This was a fun exercise in implementing a bunch of the specs I've been working on recently!

OAuth 2.1 DPoP Client ID Metadata Document Pushed Authorization Requests OAuth for Browser-Based Apps Protected Resource Metadata

Here's the link to the full ATProto OAuth docs for reference.

16:49 • 7 months ago

Wednesday, 08. October 2025

Aaron Parecki

Client ID Metadata Document Adopted by the OAuth Working Group

The IETF OAuth Working Group has adopted the Client ID Metadata Document specification!

The IETF OAuth Working Group has adopted the Client ID Metadata Document specification!

This specification defines a mechanism through which an OAuth client can identify itself to authorization servers, without prior dynamic client registration or other existing registration.

Clients identify themselves with their own URL, and host their metadata (name, logo, redirect URL) in a JSON document at that URL. They then use that URL as the client_id to introduce themselves to an authorization server for the first time.

The mechanism of clients identifying themselves as a URL has been in use in IndieAuth for over a decade, and more recently has been adopted by BlueSky for their OAuth API. The recent surge in interest in MCP has further demonstrated the need for this to be a standardized mechanism, and was the main driver in the latest round of discussion for the document! This could replace Dynamic Client Registration in MCP, dramatically simplifying management of clients, as well as enabling servers to limit access to specific clients if they want.

The folks at Stytch put together a really nice explainer website about it too! cimd.dev

Thanks to everyone for your contributions and feedback so far! And thanks to my co-author Emilia Smith for her work on the document!

Just a Theory

Sqitch 1.6.0: Now with ClickHouse!

Sqitch 1.6.0, out today, adds support for managing ClickHouse databases.

Out today: Sqitch v1.6.0. This release adds a brand new engine: ClickHouse. I started a new job at ClickHouse on September 2, and my first task, as a way to get to know the database, was to add it to Sqitch. Fortuitously, ClickHouse added support for updates and deletes, which Sqitch requires, in the August release. Sqitch v1.6.0 therefore supports ClickHouse 25.8 or later.

As for the other engines Sqitch supports, this release includes a ClickHouse tutorial, the --with-clickhouse-support option in the Homebrew tap, and Sqitch ClickHouse Docker tags.

Find it in the usual places:

sqitch.org GitHub CPAN Docker Homebrew

Thanks for using Sqitch, and do let me know if you use it to manage a ClickHouse database, or if you run into any issues or challenges.

More about… Sqitch ClickHouse

Saturday, 04. October 2025

Jon Udell

Maps old and new

I’m visiting with American friends who are staying in a rural farmhouse in France’s Dordogne valley. The house, which might be several hundred years old, provides faster internet access than my fiberoptic setup at home. The cars we are piloting along these ancient byways have touchscreens that control Bluetooth and satellite connections. It feels like … Continue reading Maps old and new

I’m visiting with American friends who are staying in a rural farmhouse in France’s Dordogne valley. The house, which might be several hundred years old, provides faster internet access than my fiberoptic setup at home. The cars we are piloting along these ancient byways have touchscreens that control Bluetooth and satellite connections. It feels like the perfect juxtaposition of the old and the new. But the illusion cracked yesterday when we headed out to visit the medieval town of Sarlat-la-Canéda. I punched “Sarlat” into the satnav and off we went, choosing the slowest but most scenic of the offered routes. As we approached the destination my friend said: “Something is wrong, Sarlat is small but it’s not this small.” You can probably guess what happened. The maps app had found a tiny hamlet 50 miles to the north instead of the populous town 30 miles to the west. Although I know better I fell for the illusion: I’m on vacation, let the machine take care of the details, we’ll just enjoy the view. Oops.

It wasn’t really a problem. We had plenty of time, we’ve been taking back roads in order to see the countryside, we just ended up seeing more and different countryside than planned. But unlike the last time I toured France, almost 25 years ago when connected phones and map apps weren’t yet a thing, I didn’t have a conventional map and neither did my friends. Had I looked at one we would never have made this error. The map on your phone isn’t really a map, it’s a tiny viewport that can see the whole planet at any resolution but never provides the context your brain needs to reason about spatial relationships. It’ll get you from point A to point B but struggles to convey where B is in relation to C.

I’m not blaming the tech, it is a miracle I will never take for granted. The fault is entirely mine for not having a real map, spreading it out on the kitchen table before we left, enjoying a beautiful and information-dense work of cartographic art, and planning the trip with the big picture in view. That would have been another nice juxtaposition of old and new. On my next GPS-guided trip to town I’ll pick up a real map: another miracle I should never take for granted.

Update: Look what we found in a drawer. Made by Institut Géographique National in 1972.

I reckon you’d need a 16K x 12K screen to view it at print resolution.

15:24 • 7 months ago

Aaron Parecki

Meetable Release Notes - October 2025

I just released some updates for Meetable, my open source event listing website.

I just released some updates for Meetable, my open source event listing website.

The major new feature is the ability to let users log in with a Discord account. A Meetable instance can be linked to a Discord server to enable any member of the server to log in to the site. You can also restrict who can log in based on Discord "roles", so you can limit who can edit events to only certain Discord members.

One of the first questions I get about Meetable is whether recurring events are supported. My answer has always been "no". In general, it's too easy for recurring events on community calendars go get stale. If an organizer forgets to cancel or just stops showing up, that isn't visible unless someone takes the time to clean up the recurrence. Instead, it's healthier to require each event be created manually. There is a "clone event" feature that makes it easy to copy all the details from a previous event to be able to quickly manually create these sorts of recurring events. In this update, I just added a feature to streamline this even further. The next recurrence is now predicted based on the past interval of the event.

For example, for a biweekly cadence, the following steps happen now:

You would create the first instance manually, say for October 1 You click "Clone Event" and change the date of the new event to October 15 Now when you click "Clone Event" on the October 15 event, it will pre-fill October 29 based on the fact that the October 15 event was created 2 weeks after the event it was cloned from

Currently this only works by counting days, so wouldn't work for things like "first Tuesday of the month" or "the 1st of the month", but I hope this saves some time in the future regardless. If "first Tuesday" or specific days of the month are an important use case for you, let me know and I can try to come up with a solution.

Minor changes/fixes below:

Added "Create New Event" to the "Add Event" dropdown menu because it wasn't obvious "Add Event" was clickable. Meeting link no longer appears for cancelled events. (Actually the meeting link only appears for "confirmed" events.) If you add a meeting link but don't set a timezone, a warning message appears on the event. Added a setting to show a message when uploading a photo, you can use this to describe a photo license policy for example. Added a "user profile" page, and if users are configured to fetch profile info from their website, a button to re-fetch the profile info will appear.
14:32 • 7 months ago

Monday, 15. September 2025

FACILELOGIN

Where the Latency Hides?

https://unsplash.com/photos/birds-sitting-on-a-power-line-7F5IzK8v8iY Effective latency optimization in complex distributed systems hinges on a simple principle: you can’t improve what you can’t measure. Having a comprehensive, multi-layered measurement baseline that offers a granular, end-to-end view of the request path helps isolate whether delays originate at the client, the network, the CDN, th
https://unsplash.com/photos/birds-sitting-on-a-power-line-7F5IzK8v8iY

Effective latency optimization in complex distributed systems hinges on a simple principle: you can’t improve what you can’t measure. Having a comprehensive, multi-layered measurement baseline that offers a granular, end-to-end view of the request path helps isolate whether delays originate at the client, the network, the CDN, the load balancer, or deep within origin services. At DevRev, we track how much latency each hop from the end user to the origin services contributes, and this allows us to consistently refine our system and deliver the best value to our customers. Today, we’ve accumulated over 10 billion latency records across different APIs, giving us unparalleled visibility into performance.

This graph shows the variation of the number of active distinct API endpoints (which handled over 8.5 billion requests) over the last 20 months. For each API request we track the latency at the edge and the origin.

By analyzing these latency datasets, we’ve uncovered patterns that aren’t always visible in small-scale tests like hidden queuing delays under burst traffic, cross-region routing inefficiencies, or subtle inconsistencies introduced by third-party dependencies. More importantly, we’ve learned that latency issues often don’t come from a single bottleneck, but from the compounded effect of several small inefficiencies across layers. In this blog, we delve into the lessons we learned while uncovering blind spots in improving end-to-end latency across DevRev API traffic.

The journey of a request from the client to the origin service is far more complex than it appears on the surface. Beneath the simplicity of a browser hitting a URL lies a carefully orchestrated chain of TCP connections and TLS handshakes that ensure secure, reliable, and performant communication. The diagram below illustrates this flow in the DevRev context, showing how requests move from the end user through edge services to the DevRev gateway at the origin, which then routes the request to the appropriate microservice. Each time a new TCP connection is established or a new TLS handshake takes place; adds latency, the impact of which also depends on how far apart the two systems are.

Beneath the simplicity of a browser hitting a URL lies a carefully orchestrated chain of TCP connections and TLS handshakes that ensure secure, reliable, and performant communication. This diagram illustrates how requests travel across the edge and origin environments before reaching the DevRev gateway, which then dispatches the request to the appropriate microservice.

The first mile of the connection is between the user’s device (the DevRev web app or mobile app) and the nearest Fastly Point of Presence (PoP). For example, the user’s browser establishes a TCP connection with the nearest Fastly PoP, along with a TLS handshake. The Fastly PoP then terminates the TLS connection and establishes a new TCP connection with the AWS Global Accelerator (GA) and a TLS connection with the AWS Application Load Balancer (ALB). The ALB, in turn, establishes another TCP connection and TLS handshake with the Istio ingress gateway, which then does the same with the DevRev gateway.

A TCP connection is established through a process called the three-way handshake, which ensures that both the client and the server are ready to communicate reliably. First, the client sends a packet with the SYN (synchronize) flag set to the server, signaling its intention to start a connection. The server responds with a SYN-ACK (synchronize-acknowledge) packet, indicating that it has received the request and is also ready to establish the connection. Finally, the client replies with an ACK (acknowledge) packet, confirming receipt of the server’s response. Once the handshake is complete, a reliable, bidirectional TCP connection is established, and data transfer can begin. The client may start sending data immediately with the final ACK. While the server is technically allowed to send data with the SYN-ACK, most implementations wait until the ACK is received before responding with application data. Roughly, the TCP handshake introduces a latency of about one Round Trip Time (RTT) between the client and the server. If you are interested in reading in detail about the TCP and the TLS handshake, this blog which I wrote in July, 2017 could be helpful.

The AWS GA operates at Layer 4 (see the diagram above), meaning it deals with TCP and UDP flows. It does not inspect application-level data. Unlike an ALB, which is a Layer 7 service that terminates HTTP/S and can make decisions based on URLs, cookies, or headers, GA is only concerned with the source IP/port and destination IP/port of the packets. It acts as a pass-through proxy, forwarding the TCP or UDP packets unmodified from the edge to the ALB. The GA helps DevRev traffic enter the AWS backbone as close to the end user as possible; however, no TLS handshake happens between the Fastly POP and the GA, but a TCP handshake; it simply acts as a pass-through.

The TLS handshake between the Fastly POP and the AWS ALB effectively takes place over two separate TCP connections (see the diagram above), with GA acting as a transparent transport-layer proxy in the middle. The first TCP connection is established between the Fastly POP and the nearest GA edge location, traversing the public internet. Once the client-side handshake completes, GA opens a second TCP connection from its edge location to the target ALB, this time over the AWS global backbone. Although GA breaks the underlying TCP session into two legs, it does not terminate or re-encrypt the TLS session. Instead, GA forwards the encrypted bytes from the Fastly POP to the ALB without decrypting them, preserving end-to-end TLS security. In other words, there is one logical TLS session (between Fastly and the ALB) but two independent TCP connections underneath it. This design allows GA to optimize routing and congestion control while maintaining full confidentiality of the TLS payload.

The cost of establishing a TCP connection is not the same in each hop, primarily due to differences in network latency and physical proximity. For example, from Bangalore to London, RTT typically ranges between 120–180 ms, carried mostly on submarine cables through the Middle East or via Singapore. Bangalore to New York is longer, usually around 220–280 ms, as the packets often traverse Europe before crossing the Atlantic. Between Bangalore and San Francisco, the RTT can stretch to 250–320 ms, depending on whether the route goes westward via the Pacific or eastward via Europe and the U.S. In contrast, London to New York averages a much shorter 70–90 ms, and London to San Francisco falls in the 140–170 ms range.

The most unpredictable handshake, in terms of latency, is typically the first one between the client and the Fastly POP, since it traverses the public internet, where RTTs are both high and variable. The next TCP connection, from the Fastly POP to the AWS GA edge location, also occurs over the public internet and is therefore subject to similar variability, though often to a lesser extent. In contrast, the TCP connection between the GA edge and the AWS ALB is significantly more stable and efficient, as it travels entirely over the AWS global backbone; an optimized, congestion-controlled network that avoids the internet weather affecting public routes. However, it can still incur noticeable latency depending on the geographical distance between the GA edge and the ALB’s regional data center.

DevRev operates in five AWS regions (USA, India, Singapore, Australia, and Germany), with plans to add more. This graph shows the p90 network latency at the edge and at the origin over 90 days for requests originating from end users in various global regions to the DevRev USA region. The edge latency reflects not just the TCP connection cost, but the overall network latency or RTT from a Fastly PoP at the edge to the DevRev Istio ingress gateway, and it excludes any latency introduced by the origin services. The origin latency reflects the complete RTT at the Istio ingress gateway and also includes the complete processing cost by the origin services.

The connections within the origin, from the ALB to the Istio ingress gateway and on to the DevRev Gateway are by far the cheapest. These handshakes occur within the same AWS Virtual Private Cloud (VPC), often with an RTT of few milliseconds, making their contribution to overall latency almost negligible compared to the initial connection from the end-user.

The most common techniques to avoid the cost of TCP and TLS handshakes focus on reusing existing connections and optimizing the handshake process itself. The primary method is connection reuse through HTTP Keep-Alives, where a client and server agree to keep a TCP connection open after a request is complete. This allows subsequent requests to be sent over the same connection without incurring the three-way handshake delay. Beyond simple Keep-Alives, connection pooling plays a critical role. Browsers, CDNs, and reverse proxies often maintain a pool of pre-established connections to the same host, enabling concurrent requests to be dispatched without the overhead of setting up new sockets.

DevRev uses Fastly as its CDN and Fastly implements a sophisticated system of connection pooling to the origin servers. At its core, Fastly’s origin connection pooling is a system of persistent TCP connections maintained at each POP. When a user request results in a cache miss, the POP connects with the origin server. This triggers the creation of a new TCP connection and a full TLS handshake. Once the origin responds and the request is complete, Fastly does not close the connection. Instead, it keeps the connection alive in an idle state within the POP’s pool. The next time that POP experiences a cache miss, it simply reuses the idle connection, bypassing the need for a fresh handshake. By avoiding these expensive roundtrips, Fastly can significantly improve the Time to First Byte (TTFB) for uncached content. At DevRev, we currently cache only static resources, while nearly all API requests are forwarded by Fastly to the origin.

A Fastly POP is made up of many cache servers (nodes), typically at least 16, with larger POPs often running 32, 64, or more. Each node maintains its own connection pool to an origin. That means if the first request to api.devrev.ai lands on node A, the connection from node A to the origin will be created and pooled on that node. If the second request lands on node B in the same POP, node B will establish its own separate connection to the origin, since connection pools are not shared across nodes within a POP.

Fastly maintains origin connection pools at the node level, keeping connections open for reuse as long as possible. When traffic is steady, subsequent requests to the same origin from a given node can reuse these pooled connections immediately, avoiding the overhead of new TCP and TLS handshakes. If a connection sits idle, however, Fastly will generally close it after a short period , typically on the order of 10–30 seconds, unless the origin itself enforces a shorter keep-alive timeout. In addition, nodes may proactively trim idle connections under resource pressure. As a result, pooled connections are highly effective during bursts of traffic but may appear short-lived from the origin’s perspective when traffic is intermittent. Fastly randomly selects which node (within a POP) a request lands on; in a low-traffic environment, this increases the likelihood that a new request will land on a different node and therefore find no pooled connection to the origin.

In the early days of DevRev, we observed a noticeable variation in latency between the Fastly POPs (at the edge) and the origin. We suspected this was largely due to the TCP/TLS handshake between the edge and the AWS ALB at the origin, as well as the way Fastly reuses connections. To validate this, we (with Brian Byrne and Steve Poitras) conducted an experiment to study how Fastly handles connection pooling. After the test, we plotted the frequency distribution against the corresponding edge latency values, which resulted in a bimodal distribution, as shown below.

This graph shows the frequency distribution against the corresponding edge latency values, which resulted in a bimodal distribution. The data points range widely, with two noticeable clusters.

This bimodal pattern clearly indicated two dominant states of connectivity. The first peak corresponded to requests that successfully reused an existing pooled connection, resulting in consistently low latency. The second peak, however, represented cases where no pooled connection was available and a fresh TCP/TLS handshake was required, adding a significant overhead to the response time. The contrast between these two peaks helped us confirm that connection reuse was indeed the main factor driving latency variation across Fastly POPs.

How can we ensure Fastly reuses connections to the origin? We discovered something we had previously overlooked. In our AWS load balancer configuration, the connection idle timeout was set to 1 minute (the default), while the HTTP client keep-alive duration was set to 1 hour (also the default). The connection idle timeout defines how long an existing client or target connection can remain inactive with no data sent or received before the ALB closes it. In contrast, the HTTP client keep-alive duration specifies the maximum amount of time the ALB will maintain a persistent HTTP connection to a client. Once this duration elapses, the load balancer processes one final request and then gracefully closes the connection.

The connection idle timeout only matters when the connection sits unused. If the client keeps sending requests within the idle window, the connection stays alive. But if the client is active, idle timeout isn’t the limiting factor for reuse. HTTP client keep-alive duration, on the other hand, is a hard cap on how long the ALB will keep a persistent HTTP connection open, even if it’s active. If this value is too low, the ALB will proactively close connections more frequently, forcing the client to set up new TCP/TLS handshakes; reducing reuse. We adjusted these values accordingly and reran the test. The resulting histogram was still bimodal, but the larger cluster had shifted to the left; an outcome that was both desirable and confirmed our assumptions.

This graph shows the frequency distribution against the corresponding edge latency values, which resulted in a bimodal distribution; where the larger cluster is shifted left; which is the desirable outcome after adjusting the values of AWS ALB client keep-alive and connection idle timeout.

Increasing the connection reusability between Fastly POPs and the ALB significantly improved end-to-end latency. However, there was another important parameter we had initially overlooked: the TLS version negotiated between Fastly and the ALB. The ALB was configured with a security policy that did not support TLS 1.3, and as a result, all TLS handshakes between Fastly and the ALB defaulted to TLS 1.2.

While TLS 1.2 is still widely used and secure, TLS 1.3 introduces major performance enhancements, particularly in terms of connection setup time. TLS 1.2 requires two round-trips to complete the handshake, while TLS 1.3 reduces this to a single round-trip, cutting the handshake latency in half. Additionally, TLS 1.3 supports 0-RTT (zero round-trip time) resumption, allowing eligible clients to send encrypted data immediately, without waiting for the handshake to complete. However, Fastly currently supports 0-RTT only for client-side connections (between the requesting client and the Fastly edge). 0-RTT is not supported for origin connections, such as those between Fastly POPs and backend servers like ALBs. As a result, while TLS 1.3 still improves latency through faster handshakes, the full benefit of 0-RTT is only realized on the client-facing side.

This graph shows the improvement in P90 latency at the edge between Fastly POPs and the Istio ingress gateway, for the requests originating from end users in various global regions to the DevRev USA region. The P90 was calculated over the 10 days before and after implementing connection pooling improvements at the Fastly/ALB layers and enabling TLS 1.3 support at the AWS ALB.

The above graph illustrates the change in P90 latency across various regions, measured before and after optimizing Fastly’s connection pooling and the TLS version used between Fastly and the ALB. Most regions saw a noticeable improvement, with Asia-South experiencing the most significant drop. Regions like EU-West and EU-East also benefited, showing moderate latency reductions. US regions (East, Central, and West) showed minor but consistent improvements, indicating already low baseline latency with slight tuning. Only EU-Central saw a marginal increase, likely within normal fluctuation. Overall, the optimization effort yielded clear performance gains across nearly all measured geographies; based on the P90 latency data collected 10 days before and after the optimization.

This graph shows the monthly p90 latency at the edge for a given API between Fastly POPs and the Istio ingress gateway, for the requests originating from end users in US-East and Asia-South regions to the US-East origin.

Fastly provides several origin-related configuration parameters that directly impact the latency observed between Fastly’s edge POPs and the origin (ALB). These parameters (max_conn, first_byte_timeout, connect_timeout, between_bytes_timeout) control how Fastly manages connections and waits for responses, and tuning them correctly is key for minimizing end-to-end latency and avoiding artificial delays.

The max_conn setting defines the maximum number of simultaneous connections Fastly can open to a backend (origin) per cache node. A Fastly POP is made up of many cache servers (nodes), typically at least 16, with larger POPs often running 32, 64, or more. For example, if max_conn is set to 200 (the default value), then a 16-node POP can have up to 3,200 simultaneous connections with the corresponding origin. If this limit is reached, especially during traffic spikes, new origin requests are queued until existing connections are freed. This queuing introduces wait time and increases latency. If max_conn is set too low, even under moderate load, Fastly may throttle itself unnecessarily, affecting responsiveness. However, setting it too high risks overwhelming the ALB or the targets behind it. Fastly also enforces an upper bound of 1,000 connections per cache node.

The first_byte_timeout defines how long Fastly waits after sending a request to receive the first byte of the response. Setting this value too aggressively can cause Fastly to abort legitimate requests and retry them. Conversely, setting it too high can lead to connection starvation, especially when dealing with slow APIs, and may result in increased latency across all APIs, as Fastly begins queuing requests once it hits the maximum connection limit.

Some time ago, we observed a latency degradation in requests routed through a specific set of Fastly POPs. When we plotted (see the graph below) the latency values across all API traffic from those POPs, we noticed a clear spike in latency levels starting on a particular date. On that same day, we had introduced a new session-handling API for a limited set of tenants. Further investigation revealed that this API was under heavy load and the backend implementation was extremely slow. As an immediate mitigation, we decided to remove the API from Fastly until the backend slowness could be addressed.

Then again, its not ideal that an issue in one service leads to a latency degradation across all the other services. This indicated a lack of sufficient isolation in our edge-layer architecture. Because all APIs shared the same Fastly backend pool and routing configuration, requests to the slow session API began to tie up connections and resources that other APIs depended on. As a result, even otherwise healthy endpoints experienced increased latency. To address this, we explored ways to introduce better blast radius containment at the edge. One approach was to isolate high-risk or low-SLA APIs into separate Fastly backends with independent connection pools and health monitoring.

This graph shows the p50, p90, and p95 latency trends across all APIs over time, for requests hitting a specific set of Fastly POPs. The spike in latency was caused by a newly introduced API that had high request volume and a slow backend.

The connect_timeout parameter determines how long Fastly waits for the TCP handshake to complete with the origin (ALB). If this is too short, brief network jitter or origin-side delays may cause timeouts and retries, introducing unnecessary latency. If it’s too long, Fastly will spend extra time waiting for doomed connections to fail, increasing mean latency. A reasonable default here is typically in the 100–500 ms range, but it should be guided by real-world network measurements between Fastly POPs and the ALB.

Finally, the between_bytes_timeout setting controls how long Fastly waits between receiving successive bytes after the response has started streaming. This parameter is particularly relevant for large file downloads, chunked responses, and streaming APIs. If the timeout is too short, temporary slowness or backpressure from the client can cause Fastly to terminate the transfer prematurely. On the other hand, if it’s set too high, it may mask genuine stalls or slow reads from the origin, leading to increased tail latency. Additionally, when a slow origin API holds onto connections for too long, it can consume the connection pool and cause Fastly to begin queuing other requests, impacting the latency of all APIs sharing that backend once the maximum connection limit is reached.

Apart from the above parameters, Fastly also enforces a limit per request execution time (wall time) of it’s Compute@Edge runtime; which is set to 2 minutes by default. This is a platform-level constraint that can only be adjusted by Fastly’s internal engineering or support teams, typically on a case-by-case basis for enterprise customers with specific workloads that require longer processing windows. This constraint applies not only to the time Fastly spends executing code, but also to the entire duration it takes Fastly to deliver the final byte to the client.

Fastly runs customer code in isolated environments at the edge, as an WebAssembly (Wasm). To ensure fairness and avoid resource abuse, it imposes this hard upper limit on how long any single request can run. Without this constraint, a poorly written or malicious piece of code could monopolize CPU and memory on a node, potentially degrading service for other customers. By bounding execution time, Fastly maintains multi-tenant safety and isolation. Almost all service proxies (including Envoy) have similar constraints.

Latency tuning isn’t just a backend problem. It’s a full-stack, full-path discipline. It involves understanding how every hop in a request from the browser to the CDN, to the load balancer, through the service mesh, and finally to the microservice, contributes to the user experience. It requires curiosity, a high degree of observability, and a willingness to revisit decisions that might look good enough on the surface. At DevRev, this effort has paid off, not just in better dashboards, but in tangible, measurable improvements to system responsiveness, customer satisfaction, and infrastructure efficiency. And most importantly, we now know where the latency hides and how to chase it down.

But this is not a one-time exercise. Latency shifts subtly over time with traffic growth, new deployments, region expansions, and the evolving behaviors of clients, CDNs, and infrastructure. With over 10 billion latency records and growing, we’re able to see not just averages, but outliers, distribution tails, and hidden bimodal patterns that would otherwise go unnoticed. This level of insight has allowed us to make iterative, and data-driven improvements.

In the end, latency is not just a technical metric, it’s a proxy for how your product feels in the hands of a user. And the only way to consistently improve it is to observe relentlessly, optimize continuously, and never stop asking: where else could it be hiding?

Disclaimer: This blog was reviewed and copy edited for clarity with assistance from OpenAI’s ChatGPT-5, while preserving the original intent.

Where the Latency Hides? was originally published in F A C I L E L O G I N on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

16:50 • 8 months ago
